ontology.foodex2_description: Noodles, cooked — used as closest FoodEx2 category anchor for stir-fried yellow noodle dish with BBQ pork.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for char siew noodles; A0FJP (cooked noodles) is the closest defensible category.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard hawker plate of char siew noodles is approximately 300 g, comprising a bed of yellow noodles topped with sliced BBQ pork, beansprouts, and spring onion, dressed with soy-based sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Char Siew Noodles — Singapore Health Promotion Board Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from HPB Foodo entry for Char Siew Noodles (cr_id F05060021). All seven reported nutrients (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre) are sourced at confidence 0.95. Micronutrients not reported by HPB are set to -1.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Yellow Wheat Noodles — analogous estimate based on cooked wheat noodles entry
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for char siew noodles as a composite dish. GI of 58 (medium) is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for cooked yellow wheat noodles, which is the dominant carbohydrate component of this dish. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (58 × 42.69) / 100 ≈ 24.8, rounded to 24.9. Carbohydrate per 300 g serving = 14.23 × 3 = 42.69 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 452 mg per 100 g; a standard 300 g serving delivers approximately 1,356 mg sodium, representing 68% of the 2,000 mg daily limit.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced sauce or sauce on the side, avoid adding table soy sauce or chilli sauce, and limit portion size to reduce sodium load from the combined soy sauce, oyster sauce, and dark soy sauce dressing.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Refined yellow wheat noodles with GI 58 (medium) and low dietary fibre (0.86 g per 100 g); a 300 g serving delivers approximately 42.7 g carbohydrate with only 2.6 g fibre, producing a glycemic load of approximately 24.9.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should limit portion size to 150–200 g, request wholemeal noodles where available, and pair with additional non-starchy vegetables such as beansprouts or bok choy to slow glucose absorption and reduce the overall glycemic load.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium (452 mg per 100 g) and char siew pork contributing saturated fat and dietary cholesterol; the soy-based sauce combination further elevates sodium beyond cardiovascular-friendly thresholds.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals managing cardiovascular disease should choose smaller portions, request less sauce to reduce sodium and saturated fat intake, and consider substituting char siew pork with leaner protein such as steamed chicken or tofu to lower saturated fat and cholesterol contribution.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Char siew (BBQ pork) is a red meat high in arachidonic acid that amplifies inflammatory pathways; combined with high sodium (452 mg per 100 g) which may worsen joint swelling and fluid retention.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should consider substituting char siew with chicken or tofu to reduce arachidonic acid-mediated inflammation, and request reduced sauce to lower sodium-driven tissue swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Char Siew Noodles (叉烧面) is a popular Singaporean Chinese hawker dish consisting of springy yellow wheat noodles stir-fried or tossed with sliced char siew (Cantonese-style BBQ pork), fresh beansprouts, and spring onion, dressed in a savoury sauce made from soy sauce, oyster sauce, dark soy sauce, and sesame oil. The dish is a staple at hawker centres and coffee shops across Singapore and is closely associated with Cantonese culinary tradition. The noodles have a firm, slightly chewy texture and a rich, umami-forward flavour profile with a hint of sweetness from the char siew marinade. Per 100 g, the dish provides 130 kcal, 7.74 g protein, 4.71 g fat, 14.23 g carbohydrate, 3.55 g sugar, 0.86 g dietary fibre, and 452 mg sodium. The high sodium content (452 mg per 100 g) is a primary clinical concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease, driven by the multi-sauce dressing. The refined yellow noodle base has a medium glycemic index of approximately 58, making portion control important for individuals with type 2 diabetes. The char siew pork component contributes saturated fat and arachidonic acid, which is relevant for individuals managing rheumatoid arthritis or cardiovascular risk. A standard hawker serving is approximately 300 g, delivering around 390 kcal, 42.7 g carbohydrate, and 1,356 mg sodium.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database at confidence 0.95 for an exact match entry (cr_id F05060021). GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for cooked yellow wheat noodles, which is the primary carbohydrate component. Micronutrient fields not reported by HPB are set to -1 and documented.
notes: Fields set to -1 (saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g): detailed fatty acid breakdown, micronutrient, and moisture data for this composite hawker dish are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for F05060021 as of 2026-04-16. No secondary approved source (USDA FoodData Central, ICMR-NIN) contains an exact match for this specific Singaporean char siew noodle preparation. Input cultural tags ('western', 'plant_based', 'low_calorie') were corrected: this dish is Singaporean Chinese cuisine, is not plant-based (contains pork), and is not low-calorie at a standard 300 g serving (~390 kcal). Glycemic load per serving recalculated as (58 × 42.69) / 100 = 24.8 g (reported as 24.9 g); input GL of 8.3 appeared to be calculated on a per-100 g basis rather than per serving and has been corrected to a per-serving figure. GI source confidence from input (0.529) reflects analogous estimation; this is documented in gi_source.
